turns.content: Hey everyone, I'm trying to set up a DMA transfer on the new board using embedded-dma but I thought since we're targeting no_std it should just work like normal std memory operations. Does the lack of std mean I can't use regular Vec or slice methods for the buffer?

Welcome to the team! You're right that we are in a no_std environment, which means standard library types like Vec aren't available by default. For DMA you need a stable address without bounds checking and the memory must remain coherent until your operation completes. The embedded-dma crate expects a slice of u8 with a fixed length so you can pass it directly to the transfer function.

Got it, I was worried about needing extra wrappers. But what about cache coherency? On my last job we had to manually flush the cache before starting a DMA and I'm wondering if there's any special handling needed here too.

You're spot on about manual flushing. In no_std setups without OS-level memory management, you must ensure the CPU cache is coherent with the DMA buffer before starting a transfer and again after it finishes. The embedded-dma crate provides a helper trait called CoherentMemory that implements the required cache flush methods for your specific peripheral.
That makes sense, so I should check if my buffer type implements that trait? But wait, what exactly is this CoherentMemory thing and how does it interact with regular memory operations?

CoherentMemory is a marker trait defined in the embedded-dma crate that signals to your code which buffers require cache maintenance. When you implement this trait for your buffer type, you can call its flush method before any DMA starts and drain after it ends. This ensures no stale data remains between CPU and peripheral.
Okay so I need to write a small wrapper around my raw pointer that implements CoherentMemory? And does this replace the need for any manual assembly instructions or something?

Exactly, you just need to implement that trait with the appropriate cache flush and drain calls. This replaces all the messy low-level assembly instructions you'd normally write manually. The embedded-dma crate handles the high-level coordination so your code stays clean while still being correct.
Perfect, that simplifies things a lot. One last thing though - since we're not using std, how do I handle error cases if the DMA fails or times out? Do I need to implement my own panic logic?

Since there's no std, you'll never get a runtime panic from the library itself. Instead, embedded-dma returns an Option or Result type that lets your code handle failures gracefully without unwinding. You can check if the transfer succeeded and log errors to your own error reporting system instead of relying on standard exception handling.
